observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reclassify the previously classified PPE items with carrying amounts from ₱15,000.00 to below ₱50,000.00 as semi-expendable property, subject to the computation of the corresponding impairment. Secondly, tangible items to be acquired in the ensuing years be properly recorded as semi-expendable property.

We also recommended that the GSO use the new forms, registries, and reports prescribed in the Circular, where necessary, to strengthen controls over the semi-expendable items.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reclassify those payments of payables, liquidation of cash advances, various JEV adjustments, and beginning balances with no supporting details that were recorded under the PPE account to their appropriate account, in order to reflect the accurate PPE balances. We further recommended that accounting adjustments be made to correct the balance of the Government Equity account.

We recommended that management conduct the disposal proceedings necessary for those PPE items that are both fully depreciated and unserviceable, to allow fair presentation of balances in the financial statements. 

Also, we recommended that management take heed and observe the updated guidelines on the disposal of government properties set by the newly issued COA-DBM Joint Circular No. 2024-1 to facilitate systematic and timely disposal proceedings which are essential to avoid further deterioration of the properties and consequent depreciation in their value.

We recommended that management strictly adhere to the provisions of COA Circular 2020-006 on the one-time cleansing of the PPE account of the Municipality, specifically, the following:

A.	Local Chief Executive:

To temporarily relieve the members of the Inventory Committee from all their regular duties to be able to complete the physical inventory of all PPEs of the LGU in due time; and

Direct the Property Unit, in coordination with the Inventory Committee, to prepare a PIP containing at least the specific assignments/duties of the Committee members, the cut-off date and a schedule specifying the dates and locations of the inventory taking activities from start up to the targeted completion of the physical inventory.

B. 	The Inventory Committee:

Conduct a complete physical count of PPE based on the guidelines and procedures provided in COA Circular 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020; and

Ensure that the physical count is recorded/documented daily in a standard Inventory Count Form (ICF), using the format in Annex A of this Circular, which shall be used as the basis in the preparation of the RPCPPE after the physical count;

Prepare and submit the Report on Physical Count of PPE (RPCPPE) covering all the PPEs of the LGU; and

Prepare and update Property records based on the results of the physical inventory and reconciled with accounting records to come up with the reconciled balances of PPE accounts to be considered as the correct balance of the LGU's PPEs.

C. 	Property and Accounting Unit

The Property and Accounting Units undertake collaborative procedures to ensure that all PPEs included in the RPCPPE are duly recorded in their respective records and that the Property Cards (PCs) maintained by the Property Unit and the PPELCs maintained by the Accounting Unit are reconciled. The reconciliation be completed within ten (10) days from rendition of the RPCPPE by the Inventory Committee.

We recommended the following:

The Municipal Treasurer provide the Municipal Accountant the certified list of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible from them as required by Section 20 of the Manual of NGAS, Volume 1;

The Municipal Accountant maintain SLs based on the certified list of taxpayers provided by the Municipal Treasurer to support the reported RPT/SET Receivable control accounts; and

Henceforth, the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant update both their records of any changes in the list of taxpayers to reflect realistic RPT/SET receivables and collections.

We recommended that management steers towards the digitalization of documents through implementing the preservation of electronic vouchers, supporting documents, and other records and reports, and their corresponding submission to the Audit Team in electronic form pursuant to existing accounting and auditing rules and regulations.

We recommended that management direct the following:

The Inventory Committee to conduct the periodic physical inventory of biological assets and submit the RPCBA;

The Office of the Municipal Accountant to prepare the appropriate adjusting entry to record the results of reconciliation of physical inventory conducted and accounting records; and

The Office of the Municipal Accountant to provide adequate disclosure in the Notes to the Financial Statement in accordance with IPSAS 27.

We recommended management to:
Send a demand letter to the former Liquidating Officer requiring her to settle the undeposited balance and report to the Audit Team immediately if deposits has been made through the submission of a copy of the deposit slip; and
Ensure that the Municipal Accountant make the necessary adjusting journal entries to reclassify the undeposited balance of the former liquidating officer from Cash – in Local Treasury account to Due from Officers and Employees account to accurately reflect the nature of the transaction in the financial statements.

We revised our recommendation that management:
file the request for authority to write-off dormant accounts pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016;
take appropriate actions on the unsettled accounts of accountable officers who are no longer connected with the LGU; and
through the Municipal Accountant ensure to reclassify accounts of accountable officers who are no longer connected with the LGU into Other Receivable account for fair presentation of the Financial Statements.

We recommended that the management ensure that the Municipal Accountant:
Make the necessary adjusting journal entries to reflect the correct balance of the cash advances accounts and to furnish the Auditor a copy of the Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) taking up the adjustments; and
Record and/or classify the cash advances based on the nature of the disbursements with due consideration on the description of the accounts provided in the Revised Chart of Accounts.

We recommended to management through the Municipal Agriculturist to inform and/or report to the GSO and Municipal Accountant all the receipt and distribution of bags of rice seeds and for the latter to record the same in the books of accounts.

We revised our recommendation that management to strictly adhere to the provisions of COA Circular 2020-006 on the one-time cleansing of the PPE account of the Municipality more specifically to:
Reconstitute the Inventory Committee and temporarily relieve the members of the Inventory Committee from all their regular duties to be able to complete the physical inventory of all PPEs of the LGU in due time;
Ensure the Property Unit, in coordination with the Inventory Committee, to prepare a PIP containing at least the specific assignments/duties of the Committee members, the cut-off date and a schedule specifying the dates and locations of the inventory taking activities from start up to the targeted completion of the physical inventory; and
Conduct a complete physical count of PPE based on the guidelines and procedures provided in COA Circular 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reclassify those payments of payables, various JEV adjustments, and beginning balances with no supporting details that were recorded under the PPE account to their appropriate account, in order to reflect the accurate PPE balances. We further recommended that accounting adjustments be made to correct the balance of the Government Equity account.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management conduct the disposal proceedings necessary for these PPE items that are both fully depreciated and unserviceable, to allow fair presentation of balances in the financial statements and take heed and observe the guidelines on the disposal of government properties as prescribed in Joint Circular No. 2024-1 dated January 30, 2024 to facilitate systematic and timely disposal proceedings which are essential to avoid further deterioration of the properties and consequent depreciation in their value.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accountant reclassify the remaining previously classified PPE items with carrying amounts from ₱15,000.00 to below ₱50,000.00 as semi-expendable property, subject to the computation of the corresponding impairment.
Secondly, we recommended that the Municipal accountant ensures that tangible items to be acquired in the ensuing years be properly recorded as semi-expendable property.

We recommended Management to ensure that the:
Municipal Accountant review and validate thoroughly the unreconciled balance of the CIP account by tracing prior year’s records and transactions, to fully identify/document the composition of the account;
Municipal Engineer to submit Status Report on Government Projects, Programs, and Activities (RGPPAs) to the Municipal Accountant together with the Certificate of Completion to transfer the whole amount of the completed infrastructure project from the CIP account to the appropriate PPE accounts
Municipal Engineer to require the concerned contractors to submit the necessary supporting documents for the processing of their claims on the unbilled portion of the completed projects; and
Municipal Accountant to reclassify completed infrastructure projects to its proper asset accounts.

We recommended that the management require the Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjusting journal entries to reflect the correct balance of the payable accounts and to furnish the Auditor a copy of the Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) taking up the adjustments. Henceforth, use the appropriate account in recording the transactions in the books with due consideration on the description of the accounts provided in the Revised Chart of Accounts.

We recommended that management ensure the following:
Municipal Accountant to conduct a thorough analysis on all recorded payables taking into consideration the recognition criteria and the validity of claims through completeness of supporting documents;
Municipal Budget Officer to determine the propriety of the unliquidated obligations including the existence of valid claims on such balances and if supported with sufficient cash backup to facilitate settlement; and
Municipal Accountant to record the necessary adjusting and/or correcting entries to arrive at the correct balance.

We recommended that the management ensure that the:
Municipal Accountant, Municipal Engineer and GSO coordinate with the concerned creditors to process their claims for the retention money otherwise require the latter and execute a waiver in favor of the LGU; and
Guaranty Deposits Payable account composed of retention money is strictly monitored and comply with the provisions of Section 6, Annex E of the updated IRR of RA 9184 and Section 98 of PD 1445.

We recommended that management ensure the following:
The Municipal Accountant set-up the RPT/SET Receivables for CY 2024 and regularly set-up accurate RPT/SET Receivables every accounting period; and
The Municipal Treasurer develop an effective tax collection campaign program and strategy and adopt collection remedies to enhance the collection of RPT/SET Receivables.

We recommended that management procure its CSEs under the Procurement Service of DBM. If the BAC desires to procure its CSEs other than the said depot on the grounds of efficiency, practicality, economic viability and/or non-availability of stocks in the depot, the procuring entity must secure a CNAS or attach proof of unavailability of stocks from such depot.

We recommended and Management agreed to strictly comply with COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009 by submitting to the Audit Team the infrastructure/non-infrastructure contracts and POs within the prescribed period of five (5) working days from its execution to prevent undue delay in the review thereof.

We recommended the management to ensure that:
The LDRRMO timely coordinate with the Municipal Budget Officer for any changes in the appropriations of each PPAs (reversion/supplemental/realignment) and effect such changes in the LDRRMFIP to ensure effective monitoring in the utilization of funds; and
The LDRRMO and Municipal Accountant coordinate with the Municipal Budget Office in reconciling the appropriation and utilization of balances reflected in their respective records to ensure efficient monitoring and accurate reporting in the movement of funds.

We recommended that the management ensure that the Municipal Accountant:
Transfer the unexpended balances of the Quick Response Fund (QRF) and MOOE of the LDRRMF to the Special Trust Fund under the account “Trust Liability-DRRM”; and
Adequately disclose the details of the prior years’ unexpended balances of the LDRRMF in the Notes to FS following the sample provided in Annex D of COA Circular No. 2012-002.
We recommended the full implementation of the remuneration of the OSCA Head in consonance with RA 9994.

We recommended the following:

The Local Chief Executive enjoin the department head’s compliance with the requirements of the circular and direct the Municipal Engineer to provide the local accountant and the general services officer with the complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects;

That thereafter, the Municipal Accountant and General Services officer prepare and maintain all required reports, records and ledgers as mentioned in COA Circular No. 2015-008; and

Lastly, the Municipal Accountant revisit the Infrastructure Assets Accounts, ascertain the estimated useful life used for each component of the asset, determine the proper valuation of the local road assets and depreciation to arrive at the correct book value of each component as of December 31, 2023.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant identify the discrepancies between the reciprocal accounts, Due from Special Accounts/Local Economic Enterprise and Due to Special Accounts/Local Economic Enterprise, reconcile the same, and effect the necessary adjustments, as warranted, to reflect the correct account balances for fair presentation	 of accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended to management the following:

Direct the Municipal Health Officer to inform and/or report to the GSO and Municipal Accountant all receipts and distribution of donated vaccines and medicines;

Create an Appraisal Committee to assess the value of these vaccines and medicines still on hand; and

Direct the Municipal Accountant to record the same in the books by debiting Drugs and Medicines for Distribution and crediting Grants and Donations in Kind accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant for CY 2023 and onwards prepare and submit on time the BRS for a timely verification and audit of the accounts and stop the practice of preparing the latter on batches.

We recommended the following to management:

Require the Municipal Accountant to submit the Monthly Trial Balance within the 20th day after the end of the month and the Quarterly Financial Statements to enable the Auditor to conduct a timely verification and audit of the accounts of the Municipality otherwise the sanctions as provided in Section 122 of P.D. 1445 be imposed; and

Submit justification for failure to submit the required above-mentioned reports.

We recommended the Management:

Direct the Office of the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC) in coordination with the Municipal Engineer and all Department Heads to ensure that the list of ongoing PPAs and those that are to be implemented during the year is prepared with complete details and submitted to the Office of the Auditor at the beginning of the year and quarterly thereafter; and

Inform the Office of the Audit Team within ten (10) days after the infrastructure project's award or before starting the program/ activity that the appropriate project signboards and public notices are already posted.

We recommended that the Budget Officer properly assess and validate the charging of PPAs to the LDRRMF and ensure that such expenditures are in accordance with the policies and guidelines provided under NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-CSC JMC No. 2014-1 to efficiently utilize the funds and to do away with PAPs that are not related to disaster risk reduction management activities.

We recommended that Management requires the GAD Focal Point System to update the GAD Database and provide gender related information with minimum GAD indicators that is matched to the biophysical, socioeconomic, and build environment components of the LGU Ecological Profile.

We recommended that management direct the following:

a) The Inventory Committee to conduct the periodic physical inventory of biological assets and submit the RPCBA;


b) The Office of the Municipal Accountant to prepare the appropriate adjusting entry to record the results of reconciliation of physical inventory conducted and accounting records; and


c) The Office of the Municipal Accountant to provide adequate disclosure in the Notes to the Financial Statement in accordance with IPSAS 27.

We recommended that Management requires the GAD Focal Point System to update the GAD Database and provide gender related information with minimum GAD indicators that is matched to the biophysical, socioeconomic, and build environment components of the LGU Ecological Profile.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to strictly observe the accounting standards on the disclosure requirements on Inventories account provided in IPSAS 12 relative to the preparation of the financial statements.

We recommended that the Management limit the charging to Representation Expenses to what is only lawfully appropriated in the annual budget and ensure that disbursement of funds is for public purposes and from available appropriations, otherwise, an overdraft in appropriations or an incurrence of obligations without appropriations may be incurred, and to avoid similar observation or suspension/disallowance of transaction in the future.
We recommended that management require the accountable officer to render the report to the COA auditor as prescribed in Sec. 98, of the GAAM Vol. I.

We recommended that management issue designation order signed by the head of the agency to all collecting officers before they are issued accountable forms and perform collecting functions for the latter to be clothed with authority to act as such.

a) that collections be remitted or deposited/liquidated intact daily or at the close of each business day and for collectors assigned to the field, where travel time from their places of assignment to the Treasurer’s Office is more than one day, turnover of collections shall be made at least once a week or as soon as the collections reach ₱5,000.00; and

b) Stop the practice of remitting only after the whole stub of ORs is consumed to avoid the possible misuse of collections or loss through theft or robbery.
We recommended the management to stop the practice of transferring cash advances from one accountable officer to another much more to those who are not duly designated and bonded.

We recommended that Management:

a) Stop granting additional cash advance to concerned officials and employees unless the previous cash advance granted them have been liquidated; 

b) Comply with and strictly observe the laws, rules and regulations regarding cash advance to lessen the accumulation of the same, such that, failure to liquidate within the prescribed period shall cause the suspension of salaries of accountable officers concerned as provided in COA Circular No. 2012-004; and

c) Retiring/ resigning/ transferring employees are not to be cleared if he/ she still has unliquidated cash advances, otherwise, the clearing official be administratively and equally liable. Also, demand letters to be sent to all accountable officers who have retired/ transferred to other offices.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant be included in the list of bondable public officers of the LGU and be properly bonded with the Bureau of the Treasury.

We recommended that the LDRRM Council properly assess the programs, projects and activities in conformity with the policies and guidelines provided in NDRRMC, DILG, DBM and CSC Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2014-1 to avoid disbursements which are not related to disaster risk reduction management activities.
We recommended that management assign a separate Responsibility Center and RC code for the GFPS which shall serve as the RC for GAD-related expenses and GAD-related financial transactions.

We recommended that Management:

a) Comply with the Property Insurance Law in insuring all serviceable properties with GSIS; and

b) Provide appropriation for the premiums of such insurance coverage with the GSIS.
We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer deposit the collections regularly and intact to avoid misuse, theft or loss of government funds, pursuant to Section 32 of COA Circular No. 2002-003.

We recommended that the non-existing or missing PPE items without available record of accountability be identified and be derecognized from the books of accounts in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 for the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts of government agencies to establish PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability.

We recommended that management through the Local School Board prepare and approve SEF Budget in accordance with the DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular (JC) No. 1, s. 2017 to attain its goals and objectives and observe transparency and accountability in the allocation and utilization of the SEF.
We recommended that management ensure that appropriation of 1% LCPC Fund be based on the IRA to avoid over-appropriation.

We recommended that the:
Municipal Treasurer deposit the collections regularly and intact to avoid misuse, theft or loss of government funds, pursuant to Section 32 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs.
The two (2) former liquidating officers to remit the amount to the Municipal Treasurer and the latter to deposit the remitted amount to the authorized depository bank.

We recommended to Management that the:
Municipal Accountant apply the perpetual inventory method and the accounting standards of recording purchases of drugs, medicines, and apparatus as Inventories, recognize the corresponding expenses for actual issuances to the units, and maintain supplies ledger cards.
General Services Officer maintain stock/property cards and regularly reconcile with the ledger cards of the accounting unit.

We recommended that the Accounting Office:
Recognize impairment loss equivalent to the outstanding balance of dormant accounts to ensure that Accounts Receivable and Loans Receivable - Others are appropriately valued at net of impairment loss as of year-end.
Request from COA authority to write-off from the books those accounts considered dormant following the procedures set forth in COA Circular No. 2016-005.
Consider timely reporting of aging of receivables to accountable officers and/or offices to coordinate past due accounts and loans and prompt follow-up and collection as they become due and demandable.

We recommended the following:
The Inventory Committee, GSO, and Municipal Accountant are requested to be guided by COA Circular No. 2020-006 in the conduct of physical count of PPE, preparation of necessary reports, reconciliation procedures, and recording in the books.
The Inventory Committee prepare and submit the RPCPPE upon the completion of the physical inventory.
Instruct all offices to maintain a separate list or inventory of PPE under their custodianship, update property records, conduct pre-count of these PPE, and submit Departmental RPCPPE to the Inventory Committee for validation during the annual physical inventory.
Delayed disposal and dropping of unserviceable property from the books.
Failure to dispose unserviceable PPE regularly exposes the property to further destruction thus may reduce their recoverable value compared if these were appraised and sold immediately. Storing scrap or unserviceable property for a prolonged duration also signifies inefficient use of available government resources.
Recommendation:
We recommended that the GSO:
1.  organize periodic disposal of unserviceable property to prevent further deterioration and loss in recoverable value, clear out hazardous waste or materials and optimize use of storage spaces; and
2. Timely submit Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP).
We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the JEV to derecognize the PPE accounts from the books after its disposal.

We recommended that the GSO Designate:
Cancel the Acknowledgment Receipts of Equipment for the issued semi-expendable property and replace these with Inventory Custodian Slips (ICS).
Use the ICS for issuance of tangible items with cost below P15,000.00 with a life of more than one year and are not considered as PPE to establish monitoring, control and accountability over them, moving forward.
Also, we recommended that the Accounting Office:
Revisit PPE accounts and reclassify the semi-expendable properties which were recognized as PPE to the appropriate inventory or expense accounts. For those items already issued to the end-users as of beginning of CY 2021, the carrying amount shall be recognized as prior period adjustment.
Apply the criteria on capitalization of PPE as prescribed in COA Circular No. 2020-006 to ensure proper classification of assets in the books.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Revise the Notes to Financial Statements to effect the recommendations stated in Column four of the Table 1;
Round-off the figures in the narrative description into thousands for consistency of presentation in the tables and in the notes; and
Submit the revised Notes to FS within 15 days from receipt hereof.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Submit justification for the delay in the submission and for not being able to submit all the required CY 2021 reports and documents.
For CY 2022 and onwards, submit the reports and documents relative to collections and disbursements of government funds on time to conduct a timely verification and audit of the accounts otherwise the sanctions as provided in Section 122 of P.D. 1445 shall be imposed.

We recommended to Management the following:
Municipal Accountant submit the monthly pre-closing trial balance for each fund for January to December 2021 transactions
For CY 2022 and onwards, Municipal Accountant prepare and submit the financial statements within the prescribed period to facilitate verification, analysis and audit of the accounts.
Municipal Budget Officer submit the monthly SAAO for January to November 2021 (both current and continuing appropriations).
For CY 2022 and onwards, the Municipal Budget Officer prepare and submit the monthly Status of Appropriations, Allotments and Obligations (both current and continuing appropriations).
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant for CY 2022 and onwards, prepare and submit on time the Monthly BRS for a timely verification and audit of the accounts.

We recommended to Management the following:
Local Chief Executive submit a Report on Infrastructure Projects for CY 2021 that the required appropriate signboards and/or public notices relative to the projects are already posted.
For CY 2022 and onwards, the Local Chief Executive inform religiously within ten (10) days after award of the infrastructure project or before the start of the program/activity that the appropriate signboards and/or public notices are already posted.
For CY 2022 and onwards, the Municipal Engineer prepare and submit the Monthly Monitoring Report for all infrastructure projects.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Stop granting additional cash advance to concerned officials and employees unless the previous cash advance granted them have been liquidated; and
Strictly monitor periodically the submission of liquidation reports to ensure timely accounting thereof and to avoid undue accumulation of outstanding cash advances.

We recommended that non-existing or missing PPE items without available record of accountability be completely identified and derecognized from the books of accounts in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 for the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts of government agencies to establish PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability.

We recommended that management require the Municipal Treasurer to adopt internal control measures to preclude the incurrence of unnecessary expenditures in the form of bank service charges and to ensure that Cash in Bank balances do not fall down below its maintaining balance.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Submit the required supporting documents for the subject petty cash fund replenishments.
For CY 2022 and onwards, examine completeness of the supporting documents for each subsequent replenishment of petty cash fund to ensure compliance with the basic documentary requirements.

We recommended to Management the following:
Submit an explanation as to why the contracts were awarded to suppliers with doubtful legal capacity.
For CY 2022 and onwards, the BAC to undertake the proper screening of the eligibility of prospective bidders which is one of its functions as enumerated in Section 12 of the 2016 RIRR of RA No. 9184.
The Municipal Accountant ensure that all transactions of the local government unit are in accordance with laws, rules and regulations as she is to take charge of both the accounting and internal audit services of the local government unit as provided in Section 474 (b) of Article Four of RA 7160.

We recommended that management require the Municipal Accountant, GSO and all those responsible in the control and issuance of fuel, oil and lubricants; preparation and, approval of trip tickets as well as authorized passenger/s to submit the hereunder documents relative to the fuel consumption totaling P0.800 million:
SSMI
Driver’s Trip Ticket
Monthly Report of Official Travel
Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption

We recommended that the Management:
Require the Property Officer to cancel all issued PARs to end-users and issue the corresponding ARE for all the Properties, Plant and Equipment issued to officials and employees who have the custody thereto; and
Keep updated on the circulars and issuances for the succeeding issuance of Properties, Plant and Equipment.

We recommended that Management formulate a systematic policy on the granting of financial assistance to determine the extent of the amount of aid to be given to individuals in need. Beneficiaries be properly screened giving priority to the less privileged constituents.

We recommended that management through the Local School Board prepare and approve SEF Budget in accordance with the DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular (JC) No. 1, s. 2017 to attain its goals and objectives and observe transparency and accountability in the allocation and utilization of the SEF.
We recommended to Management that the appropriation of 1 per cent LCPC Fund be based on the IRA to avoid over-appropriation.

We recommended to Management the following:
Local Chief Executive ensure compliance with the responsibilities provided for in paragraph 5.0 of DILG and DBM JMC No. 2017-1 to achieve desirable socio-economic development and environmental outcomes resulting to optimal utilization of the 20 per cent EDF.
MDC with the assistance of the MPDC as head of the secretariat to specifically identify/select, plan and evaluate the PAPs subject for appropriation to ensure utilization of the funds and minimize the realignments of PPAs.
Members of the MDC coordinate, monitor and evaluate the implementation of development programs and projects to ensure implementation of PPAs as required in Section 109 of RA 7160.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer deposits the collections regularly and intact to avoid misuse of government funds, pursuant to Section 32 of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System.
We recommended the Municipal Accountant to exert effort to locate errors made in the recording of transactions and to prepare the necessary adjusting entry/ies to correct this deficiency.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant strictly scrutinizes the supporting documents so that only urgent and extremely necessary travels are authorized under EO 298 and on cash advances as provided in PD 1445. In addition, the Local Chief Executive should exercise prudence in granting travel order to only LGU personnel whose position or designation appeared on the official letter of invitation of the agency or association conducting the seminar to avoid disallowance in audit in the future.

We recommended that management adheres strictly with COA Circular No. 97-002 in granting cash advances and procurements shall be properly pursued following the procedures of R.A. 9184, otherwise known as the “Government Procurement Reform Act”.

We recommended that management:
● Ceases from granting additional cash advances to officers/employees with unsettled cash advances;
● Sends immediate demand letters to employees with outstanding cash advances and require   them to immediately settle the amount granted. If liquidation is still not achieved, consider withholding their salaries or any amount due them until balances have been fully settled; and
● Requires the Accountant to monitor outstanding cash advances of every officials & employees and report the status of their liquidations to management.

We recommended that the LCE require the Municipal Accountant to draw journal entry voucher to take up accrued interest expense, and to reclassify principal loan amortizations due for the next twelve months from balance sheet date, on all long term debts to current liabilities.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant examines the validity of the supporting documents before payment shall be done to avoid possible suspensions and/or disallowance in audit and to strictly comply with the computations of benefits as provided in DBM-DOH JMC No. 1, s2012 dated November 29, 2012 as amended by DBM-DOH JMC No. 1, s2016 dated  July 15, 2016 and Implementing Rules and Regulations (IRR) of RA 9433 otherwise known as an Act Providing for a Magna Carta for Public Social Workers.
We recommended that management ensures compliance to Section 77 of RA 7160 and Commission on Civil Service (CSC) Memorandum Circular No. 17-02 dated June 24, 2002.

The Government Share for HDMF contributions of LGU employees shall be restored back to ₱100.00 per month. However, the employees may continue to contribute more than ₱100.00 provided that their Net Take Home Pay shall not be less than ₱4,000.00.

We recommended that the LGU employees involved in the approval and payment of the salary should examine the appropriateness and completeness of the supporting documents to ensure its validity, propriety and legality.
We recommended that management ensures that fund transfers to implementing agency are supported with complete documentation pursuant to COA Circular 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.

We recommended that management strictly implements the provisions of DSWD Order No. 22, as amended by Administrative Order No. 5, Series of 2008, and Section 4(2 & 6) of PD 1445. This recommendation is without prejudice to the issuance of Notices of Suspension and/or Disallowance resulting from post-audit of transactions.

We recommended that an IAS unit be established in the Municipality of Kumalarang, following the requirement under Budget Circular No. 2004-4. It should be under the direct administrative supervision and control of the head of the agency and should be organized as an independent staff unit to perform staff functions and to institute and conduct a program of internal audit for the Municipality of Kumalarang.
Make representation to the Sangguniang Bayan, for the due establishment/creation of the Internal audit Service unit, wherein the proposal should include the organizational structure, staffing, functional charts (departmental and individual), operating budget and funding source. In organizing the unit, reference should be made to DBM budget Circular No. 2004-4 (Guidelines on the Organization and Staffing of Internal Auditing Units), the Government Accounting and Auditing Manual, Volume III (Government Auditing Standards and Procedures and Internal Control System) and the National Guidelines on Internal Control Systems, which was made effective under DBM Circular Letter no. 2008-8.

We recommended that the Human Resource Management Officer and the Municipal Accountant should see to it that all the required supporting documents are complied, computations are correct and all data are properly filled up pursuant to Sections 22 and 23, Rule XVI of the Omnibus Rules on Leave.

We recommended that management refrains from allowing officials to draw cash advances for travel in excess of what is legally allowed with a view to preventing excessive and extravagant expenditures in the future. Any amount of excess claims should be refunded by the LGU officials and personnel concerned. We further recommended that management examines itinerary of travel before signing it to avoid unnecessary routes in order to minimize cost of travel. Additionally, management should see to it that the employees follow their approved itinerary of travel.

The responsible management officials were advised to revisit their own guidelines and policies on overtime pay, if any, or prepare and issue one, if none, to ensure that the rendition and payment of overtime services in the Municipality are consistent and harmonized with existing guidelines to ensure efficient and effective use of manpower resources and prevent wastage of government funds.

We recommended that the management strengthens the review process and accounting controls over transactions involving payment of meals and snacks during meetings and other official activities.  Ensure that activity design is prepared by responsible official and duly approved by approving authority to establish necessity of activities.  See to it the menus are indicated in the Purchase Request, Purchase Orders, Request for Quotations to determine the reasonableness of the price.

We recommended that the personnel concerned in the Accounting and Budget Office use the proper account codes as prescribed presently in the Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs under COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015.
As in-charge of the keeping of the books of accounts, they should update themselves with the government chart of accounts and faithfully adhere to its implementation to ensure a fair presentation of accounts in the financial statements, correct and accurate financial reports.
We also require the reclassification of account Travelling Expense to the corresponding account/s representing the actual expenses incurred.  A Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) should be prepared by the accounting unit and furnished a copy of which to COA for reference.

The Municipal Budget Officer shall strictly comply with the rules and regulations on budget augmentation and realignment as provided in Section 336 RA 7160 as reiterated in the Government Accounting and Auditing Manual.

We recommended that the LSB refrains from appropriating for non-priority expenditures in the SEF budget, especially for those that are part of the regular activities of schools, and/or the DepEd. Instead, it will allocate the Fund for the construction and repair of school buildings; facilities and equipment; educational research; and acquisition of books and periodicals. Furthermore, the LSB shall evaluate the programs/projects/activities it ought to include in the SEF budget while taking into consideration the objectives set forth in RA 7160 and RA 5447.

We reiterated our recommendation that management (a) furnish the auditor copies of delivery receipts, purchase orders and certificate of acceptance, together with all supporting documents, within the time/period; and (b) observe the proper accomplishment of all entries in the purchase orders (PO) in accordance with COA Circular No. 96-010.

We recommended that management discontinue the practice of reimbursement in the acquisition of goods and services and strictly comply with the rules and regulations of RA 9184.
In addition, the management shall draw check payments directly in the name of the supplier in conformity with existing regulations and withhold the appropriate taxes due the government.

We recommended that the GAD Focal Person, along with responsible officials, strictly comply with the provisions of PCW, DILG, DBM and NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 and COA Circular No. 2014-001 thru conducting of gender analysis and assessment so that they can formulate policies and plans which can address gender issues in their LGU and mainstream the programs, projects and activities that could solve the issues identified to attain gender equality.

We recommended that the LCE, together with the members of the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board should fully comply with RA 9003 and implement projects to be undertaken under the Municipal revised SMWP.

We reiterate our recommendation that management reconciles the PPE account balances in the FS and RRCPPE. Any discrepancy noted in the process shall be verified and if warranted the necessary adjusting entry prepared to reflect a correct balance of the accounts. We further requested management to submit RPCPPE on or before the prescribed time and in accordance with the format, indicating therein the condition of the PPEs as provided in the NGAS.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive should instruct official concerned to strictly observe the guidelines in the utilization of the 20% Development Fund pursuant to DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2011-1 dated April 13, 2011, to attain the desired socio-economic development and improvement of environmental condition for its constituents.
	
In addition, direct the KEMCO-MPC to refund immediately the amount of ₱1,000,000.00 representing the proceeds of the non-interest bearing loan received which shall be properly acknowledged by the LGU through the issuance of official receipt by the Municipal Treasurer.
observations.reason: Big bulks of unliquidated cash advances were not liquidated or refunded because the employees concerned were not anyone connected with the LGU. Some active employees have not yet liquidated their cash advances.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and all officers concerned to strictly adhere to the provisions of the Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of Republic Act 9184 in their procurement process. Also, future transactions shall observe strictly the documentary requirements enumerated in the COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.
We recommended that management procure goods and services thru competitive bidding except as may be provided by law.  The procurement process provided for in RA 9184 shall be complied with.

We recommended that management strictly adhere to the rules and regulations in the procurement of capital assets, goods and services particularly the provisions of Section 54.1 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184 and COA Circular 76-41.

We recommended the BAC and the TWG to scrutinize and evaluate the canvass documents/bid quotations carefully to promote fairness and transparency in the bidding process and obtain reasonable bid offers which are advantageous to the government, thus avoiding a similar situation in the future.

The Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to strictly adhere to the provisions of DBM Circular Letter No 2011-6 dated August 25, 2011. Further, the procurement section should prioritize acquisition of regular or ordinary office supplies through PS-DBM before resorting to the other alternative modes of procurement. 
In case of non-availability of stocks, attach to the Disbursement Vouchers the Agency Procurement Request duly acted by the PS-DBM and the Price Quotation and Stocks Availability Certification on the items which are available on stocks. In case of non-availability of stocks, check for the notation on the agency procurement request the information either out of stock or that the items are non-common office supplies.

We recommended that management instructs the Accounting Department to follow the provision on the monthly Take Home Pay of all the regular municipal employees to ₱3,000.00 and to adapt control measures in the issuance of Certification for loan eligibility.

Likewise, the Accounting Department may adopt a computerized built-in scheme in the payroll system that shall set the limit of take home pay to ₱3,000.00 in order to avoid over deductions of loans in the employee’s payroll/s.
We recommended that management develop and pass a GAD Code based on the gender issues and concerns in its locality and on the women’s empowerment and gender equality agenda of the government.
